questions.question: How many rounds of DNA replication occur in meiosis?
questions.explanation: DNA replicates exactly once, in S phase of interphase, BEFORE meiosis I begins. There is no replication between meiosis I and meiosis II.

questions.question: How many haploid cells does meiosis produce from one diploid parent cell?
questions.explanation: Meiosis I produces 2 haploid cells (separates homologous pairs); meiosis II then divides each into 2, yielding 4 haploid cells total.

questions.question: In humans, sex is determined by:
questions.explanation: Humans have 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes. XX is female; XY is male. The Y chromosome carries the SRY gene that drives male development.

questions.question: Crossing over occurs during which phase?
questions.explanation: Crossing over happens in prophase I, when homologous chromosomes pair up (synapsis) and exchange segments at chiasmata. This is a major source of genetic variation.

questions.question: What separates during anaphase I of meiosis?
questions.explanation: Anaphase I separates HOMOLOGOUS PAIRS — sister chromatids stay attached at the centromere. Sister chromatids do not separate until anaphase II.

questions.question: A heterozygous (Aa) individual produces gametes in what proportion?
questions.explanation: Mendel's law of segregation: the two alleles separate during meiosis, so a heterozygote (Aa) produces gametes in a 1:1 ratio of A to a.

questions.question: When two heterozygous parents (Aa × Aa) cross, what F2 phenotype ratio results (assuming complete dominance)?
questions.explanation: Genotype ratio is 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa. With complete dominance, AA and Aa look the same → phenotype ratio is 3:1 dominant to recessive.

questions.question: Red snapdragon × white snapdragon → all pink F1. This pattern is:
questions.explanation: Incomplete dominance: heterozygotes show an intermediate (blended) phenotype. In codominance, both phenotypes appear simultaneously (e.g. AB blood type), not blended.

questions.question: ABO blood typing demonstrates which inheritance pattern?
questions.explanation: I^A and I^B are codominant — heterozygotes (I^A I^B) display BOTH antigens on red blood cells, giving type AB blood. Both proteins are produced; neither masks the other.

questions.question: Why are X-linked recessive disorders more common in males?
choices: Males have only one X, so any recessive allele is expressed
Males' Y chromosomes also carry the genes
questions.explanation: Females (XX) need two recessive alleles to express the trait. Males (XY) have only one X — any recessive allele on it is expressed because the Y carries no second copy of the gene.

questions.question: If 30% of a DNA sample is adenine, what percentage is cytosine?
questions.explanation: Chargaff's rule: %A = %T and %G = %C. So %A = 30% → %T = 30% → A+T = 60% → G+C = 40% → %C = 20%.

questions.question: DNA polymerase III synthesizes new DNA in which direction?
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase III adds nucleotides only to the 3'-OH of the growing strand, building new DNA in the 5' → 3' direction. The template is read 3' → 5'.

questions.question: Okazaki fragments are found on which strand?
questions.explanation: Because the lagging strand must be synthesized away from the replication fork (due to the 5'→3' rule + antiparallel strands), it is built in short pieces called Okazaki fragments, later joined by DNA ligase.

questions.question: Which enzyme replaces RNA primers with DNA nucleotides?
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase I removes RNA primers and replaces them with DNA. DNA polymerase III does the main extension; DNA ligase seals the final gap between fragments.

questions.question: Why do telomeres shorten with each cell division (in cells without telomerase)?
choices: DNA polymerase makes errors at the ends
The lagging strand can't replicate to the very end
questions.explanation: After the last RNA primer is removed from the lagging strand, there's no upstream 3'-OH for DNA polymerase to extend onto. The end gap can't be filled, so the chromosome shortens slightly each round.

questions.question: Which is NOT one of the three differences between RNA and DNA?
choices: RNA uses ribose; DNA uses deoxyribose
RNA has uracil; DNA has thymine
RNA is single-stranded; DNA is double-stranded
RNA is found only in prokaryotes
questions.explanation: RNA is found in all cells. The three actual differences: sugar (ribose vs. deoxyribose), base (U vs. T), and structure (single vs. double stranded).

questions.question: What enzyme catalyzes transcription?
questions.explanation: RNA polymerase binds the promoter, unwinds DNA, and synthesizes RNA 5'→3' using the DNA template strand.

questions.question: What is the start codon, and what amino acid does it specify?
questions.explanation: Every protein starts with methionine, encoded by AUG. The initiator tRNA carrying methionine pairs its anticodon (3'-UAC-5') with the start codon.

questions.question: What happens to introns during mRNA processing?
choices: They stay in the mRNA permanently
They're spliced out before mRNA leaves the nucleus
questions.explanation: Introns are 'intervening' sequences — they're transcribed into pre-mRNA but spliced out by the spliceosome. Only exons remain in mature mRNA and reach the ribosome.

questions.question: A single-base substitution that changes the 6th amino acid of beta-hemoglobin from glutamate to valine causes:
questions.explanation: Sickle cell anemia is the textbook example of a missense substitution (GAG -> GTG, Glu -> Val). The hydrophobic valine causes hemoglobin to aggregate under low oxygen, deforming red blood cells.

questions.question: PCR doubles DNA copies per cycle. After 10 cycles, how many copies of the target sequence?
questions.explanation: Each cycle doubles, so after n cycles you have 2^n copies. 2^10 = 1024.

questions.question: Which is NOT a required PCR component?
questions.explanation: PCR is cell-free. Required: target template, heat-stable polymerase (e.g. Taq), free nucleotides (dNTPs), and two primers flanking the target.

questions.question: What is the function of the 95 degree C step in PCR?
questions.explanation: Denaturation: 95C breaks the hydrogen bonds between the two DNA strands, separating them. Then ~55C anneals primers, and ~72C extends new DNA.

questions.question: Why are 13 STR loci used in DNA profiling?
choices: 13 is the number of human chromosome pairs
Only 13 STRs exist in the genome
questions.explanation: Each STR has a count that varies between individuals. Across 13 unlinked loci, the chance of two unrelated people matching is ~1 in a trillion.

questions.question: In gel electrophoresis, smaller DNA fragments:
questions.explanation: DNA's negatively charged backbone migrates toward the positive electrode. Smaller fragments slip through the agarose gel mesh more easily, traveling farther in a fixed time.

questions.question: If nondisjunction occurs in meiosis II of one of the two cells from meiosis I, what is the outcome of the four final gametes?
questions.explanation: Nondisjunction in meiosis II affects only one of the two meiosis-I daughter cells. That cell produces two abnormal gametes (1 extra, 1 missing). The other meiosis-I daughter divides normally, giving 2 normal gametes.

questions.question: Given the DNA template strand 3'-TACGGT-5', the mRNA produced will be:
questions.explanation: RNA is complementary to the template (A-U, T-A, G-C, C-G) and antiparallel. Template 3'-TACGGT-5' -> RNA 5'-AUGCCA-3'. Note this happens to start with AUG (a start codon).

questions.question: An autosomal-recessive carrier mother (Aa) and a homozygous recessive (aa) father have a child. What is the probability the child is a carrier?
questions.explanation: Aa x aa produces 1/2 Aa (carrier) and 1/2 aa (affected). The child has a 50% chance of being a carrier, 50% chance of being affected.

questions.question: A frameshift mutation is most likely to be catastrophic because:
choices: It always creates a stop codon immediately
questions.explanation: Codons are read in groups of three. An insertion or deletion not divisible by 3 shifts the reading frame, so EVERY codon downstream is wrong - usually with an early premature stop codon.

questions.question: Why can a carrier (Aa) female with an X-linked recessive disease pass the disease to her sons but not daughters (when crossed with an unaffected X^H Y male)?
choices: Sons inherit the X from her — 50% chance of getting the diseased X^a
Mothers don't pass alleles to daughters
questions.explanation: Sons inherit Y from dad and X from mom. Mom (X^A X^a) passes X^A or X^a with equal probability - 50% chance of an affected son. Daughters get X^A from dad (unaffected) and X^A or X^a from mom - at most carriers, never affected.
